title: Variables & Types
description: Master C fundamental types, casting, NULL pointers, and const — the building blocks you'll use in every interview problem.

sections.title: Declaring variables
sections.explanation: C is statically typed — every variable needs an explicit type. The core types for interviews: **int** (32-bit), **long** or **long long** (64-bit), **double** (floating-point), and **char** (single character). Always initialize variables to avoid undefined behavior.

exercise.prompt: Declare four variables: `count` (int 42), `big` (long long 1000000000000), `ratio` (double 3.14), and `grade` (char 'B'). Print each on its own line using the correct printf format specifier.

validation.message: Each variable should print its value on a separate line
Declare each variable with its correct type

exercise.hint: Assign: count = 42, big = 1000000000000LL, ratio = 3.14, grade = 'B'. Use LL suffix for long long literals.

sections.title: Type casting
sections.explanation: C uses explicit casts with the syntax **(type)expr**. You'll need this for integer-to-float division, char-to-int conversions (ASCII values), and avoiding overflow. Use **atoi()** for string-to-int and **sprintf()** for int-to-string.

exercise.prompt: Divide int `a = 7` by int `b = 2` using a cast to get a double result. Then convert char 'Z' to its ASCII int value, and convert the string "123" to an int using atoi. Print all three results.

validation.message: Division should give 3.5, 'Z' is ASCII 90, atoi("123") gives 123
Use (double) cast for division, (int) cast for char, and atoi for string-to-int

exercise.hint: Use (double)a / b for float division. (int)'Z' gives 90. atoi(s) converts the string.

sections.title: NULL pointers
sections.explanation: In C, **NULL** represents a null pointer (typically defined as `(void*)0`). You'll check for NULL constantly with linked lists and trees. Always check `if (ptr == NULL)` or `if (!ptr)` before dereferencing. Never dereference a NULL pointer — it causes a segfault.

exercise.prompt: Create an int pointer `p` set to NULL. Print "null" if it's NULL. Then point it to variable `x` (value 42) and print the dereferenced value.

validation.message: First print 'null' for the NULL check, then print 42 after assigning
Use NULL for comparison, &x to get address, *p to dereference

exercise.hint: Compare with NULL in the if condition. Use &x to get the address of x, and *p to read the value.

sections.title: const and common constants
sections.explanation: Use **const** for values that should never change. In interviews you'll define constants for MOD arithmetic, infinity approximations, and array sizes. `INT_MAX` and `INT_MIN` are available from `<limits.h>` (included via stdlib.h in most setups).

exercise.prompt: Define three constants: `MOD` as 1000000007 (int), `INF` as INT_MAX, and `MAX_N` as 100. Print each, then print 2000000000 % MOD and whether 99 < INF (1 for true, 0 for false).

validation.message: Use const for all declarations and INT_MAX for infinity
